h1Title: Anal Fissure: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: An anal fissure is a longitudinal tear or ulcer in the anoderm — the squamous epithelium lining the anal canal — typically located in the posterior or anterior midline. Anal fissures account for approximately 15% of all colorectal outpatient visits and affect roughly 1 in 350 people per year in the United States. The defining symptom is severe tearing or burning anal pain during and after defaecation, lasting minutes to hours, frequently accompanied by bright red rectal bleeding on the tissue. The majority of acute fissures heal within 4-8 weeks with conservative measures including dietary fibre, adequate hydration, and topical anaesthetics. Chronic fissures failing conservative therapy respond to topical nitrates, calcium channel blockers, or botulinum toxin injection; lateral internal sphincterotomy achieves cure in over 95% of medically refractory cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 350 people per year in the US; lifetime prevalence estimated at 11% in the general adult population; accounts for 15% of colorectal outpatient consultations
keyStats.demographics: Equal sex distribution; most common between ages 15-40 in the general population; second peak in older adults; the most common cause of rectal bleeding in infants and young children
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in young adults aged 20-40; acute fissures most common in this group; chronic fissures more prevalent after age 40 and in constipated older patients
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global incidence data; estimates of 30-40 per 100,000 person-years from European and US population-based studies

definition: Anal fissure (ICD-10: K60.2 for unspecified; K60.0 for acute; K60.1 for chronic) is a painful linear ulceration of the squamous epithelium of the anal canal, extending from the dentate line distally and most commonly located in the posterior midline (90% of cases) or, less frequently, the anterior midline (10% of cases, more common in women after childbirth). Fissures are classified as acute (present less than 6-8 weeks, typically a shallow laceration without indurated edges) or chronic (present over 8 weeks, characterized by indurated edges, an exposed white circular internal anal sphincter at the base, a sentinel skin tag distally, and a hypertrophied anal papilla proximally). The underlying mechanism in most cases is pathologically elevated internal anal sphincter (IAS) tone — either as a primary cause or as a perpetuating factor — which reduces mucosal blood flow to the posterior commissure (the least vascular point of the anal canal), creating ischemia that prevents healing. Lateral fissures, multiple fissures, or fissures in atypical locations should raise suspicion for secondary causes including Crohn disease, sexually transmitted infections (HIV, syphilis, gonorrhoea), tuberculosis, or malignancy. The condition is primarily managed by gastroenterologists, colorectal surgeons, and in acute cases by primary care.

typesClassification.description: Present for less than 6-8 weeks; shallow mucosal tear with relatively smooth edges and no induration. Most heal spontaneously within 4-8 weeks with conservative management including fibre, stool softeners, and warm sitz baths.

Present for over 8 weeks; develops indurated fibrotic edges, a sentinel skin tag (sentinel pile) at the external margin, an exposed white muscle floor (internal anal sphincter), and a hypertrophied anal papilla internally. Requires topical pharmacotherapy or procedural intervention for healing.

Caused by or associated with an identifiable underlying condition: Crohn disease (may be lateral or multiple, atypical appearance), sexually transmitted infections (herpes simplex, syphilis, gonorrhoea, HIV), anal cancer, or prior anal surgery. Treatment of the secondary cause is central to management.

Anterior midline fissures are more common in women following vaginal delivery, related to perineal trauma, constipation in the peripartum period, and reduced sphincter blood flow. Often resolves with the acute postpartum constipation, but may require topical treatment.

primarySymptoms: Severe anal pain during defaecation — often described as tearing, cutting, or burning — the most prominent symptom, occurring with virtually every bowel movement in symptomatic disease.

Prolonged post-defaecatory pain lasting 30 minutes to several hours, caused by internal anal sphincter spasm triggered by the act of defaecation; this protracted pain distinguishes fissures from haemorrhoids.
Bright red rectal bleeding on the toilet paper or on the surface of the stool — typically a small streak or spot, not mixed into the stool, occurring in 50-70% of acute fissures.
Fear of defaecation (defaecophobia) causing voluntary stool withholding, which worsens constipation and perpetuates the cycle of fissure injury — a critical component of chronicity.
Visible or palpable skin tag at the anal margin (sentinel pile) in chronic fissures, often misidentified by patients as a haemorrhoid.
Anal pruritus and perianal discharge from the ulcerated surface and any associated hypertrophied anal papilla secretion.
Constipation or altered bowel habit — either as a cause (hard stool triggering the fissure) or consequence (voluntary avoidance of defaecation) of the condition.
Difficulty sitting comfortably for extended periods, particularly on hard surfaces, from the sensitised and spastic anal canal.
earlyWarningSigns: Sharp anal pain on defaecation without rectal fullness or tenesmus — distinguishes fissure from anorectal abscess or thrombosed haemorrhoid, which are tender at rest.
Bright red rectal bleeding on tissue rather than dripping into the pan — surface bleeding from a mucosal tear rather than haemorrhoidal bleeding, which may drip.
Recognizable skin tag appearing at the anal margin after a particularly painful episode of passage of a hard stool.
Constipation over several weeks associated with progressive pain on defaecation — hard stools are the most common precipitant.
emergencySigns: Fever, severe perianal swelling, throbbing pain at rest, and systemic unwell symptoms — suggests anorectal abscess, which is a surgical emergency requiring incision and drainage.
Profuse rectal bleeding or haematochezia with haemodynamic instability — not typical of fissure; requires emergency colorectal and GI evaluation to exclude other bleeding sources.

New anal pain and discharge in an immunocompromised patient, or in an individual with HIV or history of anal intercourse — secondary infection (herpes, syphilis, gonorrhoea) requires specific testing and treatment.
Rapid onset of severe anal pain in a patient with known Crohn disease — Crohn-related fissure is managed differently (avoid sphincterotomy; treat underlying Crohn disease).

causes.cause: Passage of hard or large-calibre stools
causes.description: The most common precipitating cause, stretching the anoderm beyond its elastic limit and producing a linear tear. Hard stools from dietary fibre deficiency, inadequate fluid intake, sedentary lifestyle, or constipating medications account for the majority of acute fissures in adults. Recurrent hard stools prevent healing and convert acute to chronic fissure.

Elevated resting internal anal sphincter pressure — present in the majority of patients with chronic anal fissure — reduces blood flow to the posterior commissure, the least vascularised point of the anal canal. Ischaemia prevents mucosal healing. Whether hypertonia precedes or follows fissure formation is debated; it is both a cause and consequence of the condition. Reducing sphincter tone is the rationale for medical and surgical treatment.

Frequent passage of liquid stool — particularly in Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, or acute infectious diarrhoea — can cause repeated maceration and microtrauma of the anoderm, producing fissures through a different mechanism than hard stools. Crohn-related fissures tend to be lateral, multiple, and large, with atypical features.

Anal fissures are common postpartum, particularly anterior midline, related to sphincter stretch during labour, constipation in the puerperium, and perineal tissue ischaemia. They usually resolve once bowel habits normalise, but may become chronic without treatment.

The posterior commissure of the anal canal is a relative watershed zone with less subcutaneous vasculature than the anterior or lateral walls, explaining the 90% posterior midline predominance of fissures. Any process further reducing anal blood flow — elevated sphincter tone, vascular disease, or constipation — precipitates or perpetuates fissuring at this site.

causes.cause: Underlying anorectal pathology or prior surgery
causes.description: Haemorrhoidectomy, lateral internal sphincterotomy for prior fissure, or any anorectal surgery can disrupt blood supply and skin integrity, predisposing to recurrent fissures. Prior pelvic surgery or radiation may also impair anorectal vascularity.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic constipation and low dietary fibre
riskFactors.description: Dietary fibre below 20-25 g per day and low fluid intake produce hard, bulky stools that traumatise the anoderm on passage. Increasing fibre to 25-35 g per day and fluids to 8 glasses per day reduces recurrence by approximately 50% in controlled studies.

riskFactors.factor: Diarrhoeal conditions (Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, IBS)
riskFactors.description: Frequent loose stools cause repeated microtrauma. Crohn disease-associated fissures are particularly refractory and require a different treatment approach — lateral sphincterotomy should be avoided because of impaired wound healing and risk of incontinence.

Anal fissure risk increases approximately 2-3-fold following vaginal delivery, particularly with perineal tears or episiotomy. Peripartum constipation management with stool softeners and adequate hydration reduces the risk.

Physical inactivity slows colonic transit and promotes constipation. Exercise equivalent to 30 minutes of brisk walking five days per week accelerates transit and reduces constipation-related fissure risk.

HIV-positive individuals and those on immunosuppressive therapy are at higher risk of atypical, multiple, and non-healing anal fissures, including herpes simplex, syphilitic, and cytomegalovirus-related ulcers. CD4 count-driven immunocompromise predicts severity.
Previous haemorrhoidectomy, sphincterotomy, or anorectal surgery can alter blood supply and skin architecture, predisposing to recurrent fissures and complicating management.

affectedDemographics: Equal sex distribution overall; women are disproportionately affected by anterior midline fissures following childbirth.
Peak incidence in young adults aged 20-40, possibly related to higher rates of constipation-provoking diets and physical activity patterns.
Infants and young children: the most common cause of rectal bleeding in infants, typically from passage of hard stools; anterior fissures more common in this age group.
Older adults: chronic constipation from reduced dietary fibre, diminished mobility, and polypharmacy increases fissure incidence and chronicity.
Individuals with inflammatory bowel disease: Crohn disease patients have a particularly high rate of complex, atypical, multi-site perianal fissures.
diagnosisOverview: Anal fissure is a clinical diagnosis based on history and inspection; the characteristic history of defaecatory pain and bleeding with a visible or palpable tear at the posterior (or anterior) midline is usually sufficient. Detailed anorectal examination must be conducted sensitively because even gentle palpation of the anal area is intensely painful during an acute fissure. A focused visual inspection with gentle separation of the buttocks allows the fissure to be seen; a rectal examination or rigid anoscopy is deferred in the acute setting due to pain and because it rarely adds diagnostic information. Inspection reveals the acute fissure as a white-floored longitudinal tear, or the chronic fissure with its characteristic sentinel pile, exposed white internal sphincter, and papilla. Anorectal manometry may confirm elevated internal anal sphincter resting pressure in chronic cases but is rarely needed for clinical management. Endoscopy or colonoscopy is indicated in patients over 40 with new rectal bleeding, those with atypical features suggesting inflammatory bowel disease or sexually transmitted infection, and in patients who are immunocompromised. In Crohn disease, perianal disease examination under anaesthesia combined with pelvic MRI assesses fistula and abscess complications that frequently co-exist with Crohn fissures. Microbiological swabs are taken when secondary infection (herpes, syphilis, gonorrhoea) is possible.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary diagnostic test; directly visualises the fissure in the posterior or anterior midline; no instruments required
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician gently separates the gluteal folds with the patient in lateral decubitus or prone position. A white-floored linear ulcer is visible in the midline. No preparation required. Takes 30 seconds. Very painful in acute fissure — patient can stop at any time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis, excludes haemorrhoids, and assesses the anal canal including the dentate line; deferred until pain is partially controlled
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small rigid tubular scope is passed into the anal canal. Usually performed with topical local anaesthetic cream applied 30-60 minutes before. Takes 2-3 minutes. Deferred in acute fissure until topical therapy has reduced spasm.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures internal and external sphincter resting and squeeze pressures; confirms hypertonia in chronic fissure; guides treatment decisions and predicts continence risk before sphincterotomy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin pressure-sensing catheter is placed in the rectum while the patient alternately relaxes and squeezes. Takes 15-20 minutes. Results available same day. Referral to a specialist anorectal physiology unit required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Indicated for atypical fissures, rectal bleeding in patients over 40, or suspected inflammatory bowel disease or malignancy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bowel preparation required (laxatives the day before). Performed under sedation; 15-30 minutes. Biopsies can be taken from suspicious lesions. Deferred until acute anal pain is adequately controlled.

diagnosticTests.test: Microbiological swabs and serology for STIs
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes herpes simplex, syphilis, gonorrhoea, and chlamydia as causes of lateral or atypical ulcers
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Swabs taken from perianal ulcers and the anal canal. Blood tests for syphilis serology and HIV. Results in 3-7 days. Important in sexually active individuals with lateral or multiple ulcers.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is staged by acuity and response to prior therapy. The first-line approach for acute fissures is conservative: dietary fibre 25-35 g per day, adequate hydration (8 glasses of water daily), topical anaesthetic (lidocaine 2-5% gel applied before and after defaecation), warm sitz baths 10-15 minutes two to three times daily to relax sphincter spasm, and stool softeners (docusate sodium or lactulose) to prevent hard stool recurrence. This approach heals 60-70% of acute fissures within 4-8 weeks. For chronic fissures failing 4-8 weeks of conservative management, pharmacological sphincter relaxation is added. The 2020 ASCRS Practice Parameters and the NICE Interventional Procedures guidelines both recommend topical glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 0.4% ointment applied twice daily for 6-8 weeks as the most widely used first-line pharmacotherapy, achieving healing in 50-60% of chronic fissures; headache limits tolerability in up to 40% of patients. Topical diltiazem 2% ointment (applied twice daily) has equivalent efficacy with fewer headaches and is an acceptable alternative. Botulinum toxin A (20-50 units injected into the internal anal sphincter under local anaesthesia) is an effective second-line procedure, achieving healing in 65-75% at 3 months with a low risk of temporary anal incontinence (5-10%); it can be repeated. Lateral internal sphincterotomy (LIS) is the gold-standard surgical procedure for refractory chronic fissures: division of the distal third of the internal anal sphincter under general or local anaesthesia achieves healing in 90-97% of cases with permanent incontinence rates below 2% in careful hands. LIS is contraindicated in Crohn disease because of impaired wound healing.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical glyceryl trinitrate 0.4% ointment (GTN) applied twice daily for 6-8 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Nitric oxide donor that relaxes the internal anal sphincter by increasing cyclic GMP. Applied at the anal verge with a finger cot or small applicator twice daily. Headache from systemic nitrate absorption occurs in 30-40% and limits adherence; starting with 0.2% and upward-titrating reduces this.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fissure healing in 50-60% of chronic cases vs 30-35% placebo; meta-analysis of 30 RCTs; significantly lower healing rate than LIS but avoids surgical risk

medicalTreatments.name: Topical diltiazem 2% ointment applied twice daily for 8 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium channel blocker reducing internal anal sphincter resting tone. Applied as GTN but with fewer headache side-effects. Preferred in patients intolerant of GTN headache. Oral nifedipine 20 mg twice daily is an alternative when topical preparations are unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Healing in 65-70% of chronic fissures; non-inferior to GTN in direct comparative trials with better tolerability

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A injection (20-50 units) into internal anal sphincter
medicalTreatments.description: Injected into the IAS bilaterally (or posteriorly) under local or light sedation; temporarily paralyses sphincter smooth muscle for 3-6 months, allowing fissure to heal. Effect wanes as neuromuscular transmission recovers. Can be repeated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Healing in 65-75% at 3 months; 10-20% relapse at 1 year requiring repeat injection; temporary incontinence in 5-10%

medicalTreatments.name: Stool softeners (docusate sodium 100 mg twice daily; lactulose 15-30 mL daily) and osmotic laxatives
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce stool hardness and straining, removing the primary mechanical cause. Used throughout all treatment stages; essential to prevent recurrence even after pharmacological or surgical healing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combination of fibre plus stool softener heals 60-70% of acute fissures within 8 weeks; prevents recurrence in approximately 50% when maintained long-term

medicalTreatments.name: Topical lidocaine 2-5% gel applied 15-30 minutes before defaecation
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces defaecatory pain and the anxiety cycle that leads to voluntary stool withholding. Does not treat the underlying cause but breaks the pain-avoidance-constipation cycle.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No evidence for fissure healing; substantial pain relief allowing normal defaecation — an important adjunct to all other therapies

surgicalOptions.name: Lateral internal sphincterotomy (LIS)
surgicalOptions.description: Division of the distal third of the internal anal sphincter under general or local anaesthesia, typically as a day procedure through a 1-2 cm lateral incision. Reduces sphincter hypertonia permanently, restoring blood flow to the posterior commissure. Open or closed technique; outcomes equivalent.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Healing in 90-97% of chronic fissures; recurrence 2-5% at 5 years; permanent continence disturbance (minor incontinence to flatus or liquid stool) in 1-2% of cases at specialist centres

surgicalOptions.name: Fissurectomy (surgical excision) with or without anal advancement flap
surgicalOptions.description: Excision of the chronic fissure with its fibrotic edges, sentinel pile, and hypertrophied papilla; healing by secondary intention or by anoderm advancement flap. Used when LIS is contraindicated (Crohn disease, low resting pressure, prior sphincter injury) or when fissurectomy is combined with LIS for en-bloc excision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Healing in 80-90% with advancement flap for selected cases; primary fissurectomy without flap has lower healing rates (60-70%) but is simpler and avoids incontinence risk

alternativeTreatments.name: Warm sitz baths (10-15 minutes, two to three times daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Immersion in warm water reduces IAS spasm, provides analgesia, and promotes perianal hygiene. A Cochrane review found no direct evidence for fissure healing but high patient satisfaction for pain control; universally recommended as part of conservative management and peri-procedural care.

whySeeSpecialist: A colorectal surgeon or gastroenterologist should evaluate fissures that fail 4-8 weeks of conservative management, have atypical features (lateral, multiple, non-midline), or are associated with inflammatory bowel disease. Specialist input is required before botulinum toxin injection or sphincterotomy to assess sphincter function with anorectal manometry, since low baseline sphincter pressures — particularly in postpartum women or the elderly — indicate significant incontinence risk from LIS.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a colorectal surgeon performing more than 30-50 sphincterotomies per year; surgical volume is strongly associated with lower incontinence rates. For Crohn-associated or atypical fissures, a dedicated inflammatory bowel disease service with colorectal surgery partnership provides the most informed management. Botulinum toxin injection is available at most tertiary colorectal units.
hospitalCriteria: Anorectal physiology laboratory for manometry before sphincterotomy
Day surgery or procedure unit for botulinum toxin injection
Colorectal surgical programme with experience in LIS and fissurectomy
Inflammatory bowel disease service for Crohn-associated perianal disease
Dermatology or infectious disease input for atypical or STI-related perianal ulcers
keyFacilities: Day-case surgical units for botulinum toxin injection and LIS
Inflammatory bowel disease services for Crohn perianal disease
Primary care with ability to prescribe GTN and diltiazem topical preparations
preventionStrategies: Consume 25-35 g of dietary fibre daily from fruits, vegetables, legumes, and whole grains to produce soft, easily passed stools that do not traumatise the anoderm.
Drink at least 8 glasses (2 litres) of water daily — adequate hydration is as important as fibre in preventing hard stools.
Respond to the urge to defaecate promptly rather than postponing — retained stool becomes harder and more traumatic to pass.
Treat constipation proactively in pregnancy and the postpartum period with osmotic laxatives and stool softeners rather than awaiting a fissure to develop.
Avoid prolonged straining at stool — limit each toilet sitting to under 5 minutes; straining worsens sphincter blood-flow compromise at the posterior commissure.
lifestyleModifications: Increase dietary fibre to 25-35 g per day using psyllium husk, flaxseed, oat bran, or a soluble-fibre supplement if dietary sources are insufficient.
Take any prescribed stool softener or osmotic laxative consistently — skip days lead directly to hard stools and re-injury of the healing fissure.
Apply topical anaesthetic gel (lidocaine 2-5%) 15-30 minutes before each bowel movement to break the pain-avoidance cycle during treatment.
Use warm sitz baths for 10-15 minutes after each bowel movement to relax sphincter spasm and clean the area.
Apply prescribed topical GTN or diltiazem ointment at the exact times and dose specified — irregular application allows sphincter tone to recover and prevents healing.
Return to normal defaecation habits without voluntary stool withholding — even if painful, avoidance worsens constipation and perpetuates fissure chronicity.
Report persistent symptoms beyond 8 weeks of conservative treatment to a clinician — specialist evaluation is needed to assess for atypical causes and to initiate procedural therapy.

recommended: High-fibre foods at every meal: psyllium husk porridge or supplements (1-2 tablespoons daily), legumes (lentils, chickpeas), berries, apples with skin, and oat-based cereals
Plenty of water — at least 8 glasses per day; fibre is ineffective at softening stool without adequate fluid
Fermented foods (natural yogurt, kefir) to support gut microbiome and regular colonic transit
Prunes or prune juice (2-4 prunes daily) as a natural osmotic stool-softening agent rich in sorbitol
avoid: Low-fibre processed foods (white bread, white rice, pastries) that produce hard, compact stools
Alcohol and caffeine which contribute to dehydration and harder stools
Dairy-heavy or low-residue diets during active fissure management until healing is confirmed
Chilli, spices, and acidic foods that may exacerbate perianal burning in sensitive patients during acute symptoms

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise — 30 minutes of walking, cycling, or swimming five days per week — accelerates colonic transit, reducing constipation and hard stools that re-injure the anal canal. Exercise should be avoided during acute severe pain until topical therapy reduces sphincter spasm. Cycling on narrow hard saddles may increase perianal pressure temporarily; wider saddles or gel seat pads are helpful during active fissure healing.
dailyManagement: Apply topical GTN or diltiazem at scheduled times — morning and evening are standard; do not skip doses.
Take prescribed stool softener or fibre supplement with every evening meal to ensure soft stool for the next morning's bowel movement.
Use warm sitz bath for 10-15 minutes after each bowel movement to relax sphincter spasm and clean the area.
Apply topical anaesthetic 15-30 minutes before any anticipated bowel movement if pain is still significant.
Attend follow-up review at 6-8 weeks of topical therapy to assess healing response and plan escalation if needed.
prognosis: The majority of anal fissures resolve without procedural intervention. Acute fissures treated with conservative measures heal in 60-70% within 4-8 weeks. Chronic fissures treated with topical GTN or diltiazem heal in 50-70% over 6-8 weeks, with recurrence rates of 20-40% within 12 months in those who do not address the underlying constipation. Botulinum toxin injection heals 65-75% with a 10-20% one-year recurrence. Lateral internal sphincterotomy provides definitive treatment in 90-97% of cases with very low recurrence (2-5% at 5 years) and, in experienced hands, a permanent incontinence rate below 2%. Crohn-related fissures have a more variable prognosis — dependent on control of the underlying inflammatory disease — and sphincterotomy is associated with higher complication rates. Quality of life improves dramatically after successful treatment; the fear and pain cycle that severely restricts daily activities resolves with fissure healing.
recoveryTimeline: Acute fissure pain typically improves within 2-4 weeks of conservative management. Full healing of acute fissures expected within 4-8 weeks. Chronic fissures on topical GTN or diltiazem heal over 6-8 weeks. After botulinum toxin injection, pain relief is usually felt within 1-2 weeks and full healing at 6-8 weeks. After lateral internal sphincterotomy, significant pain improvement within 2-5 days; complete wound healing within 4-8 weeks.
complications: Chronicity: 30-50% of acute fissures become chronic without adequate treatment; chronic fissures develop indurated edges and sentinel tags, requiring escalated therapy.

Incontinence after sphincterotomy: permanent disturbance of continence to flatus or liquid stool occurs in 1-2% of LIS procedures at high-volume specialist centres; up to 10-20% at lower-volume centres.
Recurrence after non-surgical treatment: 20-40% of fissures healed with topical medication recur within 12 months, particularly if underlying constipation is not corrected.
Anal fistula: a fistulous tract can develop from the base of a chronic fissure, detected by MRI or EUA; requires separate surgical management and should not be confused with primary fissure disease.

Misdiagnosis and delayed cancer diagnosis: anal or rectal cancer can occasionally present with symptoms resembling chronic fissure; atypical appearance, failure to heal, or induration warrants biopsy.

Worsening constipation from defaecophobia: the pain-avoidance cycle leads to progressive constipation and impacted stool, which increases fissure trauma and complications including overflow diarrhoea from faecal impaction.

supportResources.name: American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgeons (ASCRS) Patient Education
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on anal fissure diagnosis, treatment options, and when to seek surgery.

supportResources.name: Core — Charity for Gut and Liver Disease
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information on colorectal and anal conditions including fissure management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anorectal abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anorectal abscess causes constant throbbing pain at rest, tender fluctuant swelling palpable at the anal margin or deeper, and often fever. Anal fissure causes pain provoked specifically by defaecation, with relief between episodes; there is no palpable swelling and no fever. Abscess requires incision and drainage urgently; fissure requires conservative or topical therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anal fistula
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anal fistula is an abnormal tract connecting the anal canal to perianal skin, presenting with chronic intermittent discharge of pus or blood from a visible perianal opening. It does not produce the characteristic defaecatory tearing pain of anal fissure. Fistulas require surgical fistulotomy or seton placement; fissures are treated medically or with sphincterotomy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS causes intermittent abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habit without anorectal structural pathology. Anal fissure causes localised posterior midline anal pain directly related to defaecation, with bleeding on tissue and a visible tear. Rectal examination distinguishes the two conditions immediately.

relatedConditions.name: Anal abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: Perianal abscess is in the differential for anorectal pain and may coexist with or complicate a chronic anal fissure, particularly in Crohn disease.

relatedConditions.name: Anal fistula
relatedConditions.relevance: A fistulous tract can develop from the base of a chronic anal fissure; both require colorectal surgical evaluation and may coexist in Crohn perianal disease.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: IBS-associated constipation is one of the leading precipitants of anal fissure; managing the underlying bowel habit disorder prevents fissure recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared gastroenterology specialty management; both conditions have dietary modification as a central component of prevention.

faqs.question: What is an anal fissure?
faqs.answer: An anal fissure is a small tear in the lining of the anal canal, usually at the posterior midline. It causes severe pain during and after bowel movements and often produces a small streak of bright red blood on the tissue. Most acute fissures heal within 4-8 weeks with fibre, hydration, and topical treatments.

faqs.question: What does an anal fissure feel like?
faqs.answer: The pain is typically described as sharp tearing or burning during defaecation, followed by a dull ache or spasm that may last 30 minutes to several hours. Even anticipation of a bowel movement can cause anxiety. Between episodes there is usually no pain, which distinguishes a fissure from an abscess.

faqs.question: How do you treat an anal fissure at home?
faqs.answer: Home treatment for acute fissures includes increasing dietary fibre to 25-35 g daily, drinking at least 8 glasses of water, applying topical lidocaine gel before bowel movements to reduce pain, using warm sitz baths for 10-15 minutes after each bowel movement, and taking stool softeners. These measures heal 60-70% of acute fissures within 4-8 weeks.

faqs.question: Can an anal fissure heal on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acute fissures heal spontaneously in 60-70% of cases within 4-8 weeks with dietary fibre, hydration, and sitz baths. Fissures that persist beyond 8 weeks are termed chronic, develop indurated edges, and require topical medication (GTN or diltiazem) or a procedure to break the spasm cycle preventing healing.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for a chronic anal fissure?
faqs.answer: Chronic anal fissures are treated with topical glyceryl trinitrate 0.4% or diltiazem 2% ointment applied twice daily for 6-8 weeks. If these fail, botulinum toxin injection into the internal sphincter heals 65-75% of cases. Lateral internal sphincterotomy is the definitive surgical treatment, achieving healing in over 90% with very low incontinence risk in experienced hands.

faqs.question: How long does an anal fissure take to heal?
faqs.answer: Acute fissures typically heal within 4-8 weeks with conservative management. Chronic fissures treated with topical GTN or diltiazem heal over 6-8 weeks. After botulinum toxin injection, healing takes 6-8 weeks. After lateral internal sphincterotomy, pain relief is usually felt within 2-5 days and complete healing occurs within 4-8 weeks.

faqs.question: What is the difference between an anal fissure and haemorrhoids?
faqs.answer: Anal fissure causes tearing pain during and after defaecation from a mucosal tear, with a small streak of blood on the tissue and often a skin tag at the anal verge. Haemorrhoids cause a different pain profile — typically painless bleeding from internal piles or persistent anal ache and itching from external haemorrhoids. Examination distinguishes the two.

faqs.question: Does an anal fissure cause bleeding?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bright red blood on the toilet paper or on the surface of the stool is present in 50-70% of anal fissures. The blood does not mix into the stool. Bleeding from a fissure is typically a small streak; larger volumes or blood mixed in the stool suggest a different source and warrant further investigation.

faqs.question: What causes an anal fissure?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is passage of a hard or large stool that stretches the anoderm beyond its elastic limit. Diarrhoeal conditions such as Crohn disease can also cause fissures through repeated mucosal trauma. Elevated internal anal sphincter tone reduces blood flow to the posterior commissure, the least vascular point, preventing healing in chronic cases.

faqs.question: Can anal fissures come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. After topical pharmacotherapy (GTN or diltiazem), recurrence occurs in 20-40% within 12 months, particularly if constipation is not corrected. After botulinum toxin, 10-20% relapse within one year. After lateral internal sphincterotomy, recurrence is only 2-5% at 5 years — the most durable outcome. Long-term fibre and hydration minimise recurrence risk.

faqs.question: Is surgery necessary for an anal fissure?
faqs.answer: Surgery (lateral internal sphincterotomy) is only needed for chronic fissures that have not responded to 8-12 weeks of topical therapy and botulinum toxin injection. Most patients are managed without surgery. When needed, LIS is a short day-case procedure with over 90% healing rates and very low complication rates.

faqs.question: Can diet prevent anal fissures?
faqs.answer: Yes. A high-fibre diet (25-35 g daily) combined with at least 2 litres of water per day produces soft stools that do not traumatise the anal canal. Studies show that fibre supplementation reduces acute fissure recurrence by approximately 50%. Prompt treatment of constipation with dietary measures or stool softeners is the most important preventive strategy.

faqs.question: What is lateral internal sphincterotomy?
faqs.answer: Lateral internal sphincterotomy is a day-case surgical procedure in which the distal third of the internal anal sphincter muscle is divided through a small lateral incision, permanently reducing the sphincter tone that prevents fissure healing. It achieves healing in 90-97% of chronic fissures with permanent continence disturbance in fewer than 2% of cases at specialist centres.

faqs.question: Can anal fissures occur in children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anal fissure is the most common cause of rectal bleeding in infants and young children. It is typically caused by constipation or hard stools and is usually located anteriorly in this age group. Most fissures in children heal quickly with dietary changes, stool softeners, and topical anaesthetic. Surgery is very rarely required in the paediatric age group.

faqs.question: Are anal fissures related to Crohn's disease?
faqs.answer: Crohn disease causes a specific type of perianal fissure that tends to be lateral (not midline), multiple, large, and irregular with oedematous skin tags. These fissures are treated by optimising control of the underlying Crohn inflammation rather than by sphincterotomy, which is contraindicated because of poor wound healing and increased incontinence risk in this setting.

metaTitle: Anal Fissure: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Anal fissure: painful anal canal tear causing tearing pain and rectal bleeding on defaecation. Symptoms, GTN cream, botulinum toxin and surgery reviewed.
